skill.name: allra-error-handling
skill.description: Defines Allra backend standards for Java exception classes, global error handling, error responses, and logging practices.
skill.summary: Apply Allra backend error handling standards for Java services.
skill.author: Allra-Fintech

security_audit.summary: The static external command findings are false positives caused by markdown code fences in SKILL.md, not executable shell or Ruby code. The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are also false positives from normal prose or Java examples. A moderate issue remains because examples show rejected values and email addresses in responses or logs, which can expose personal data if copied directly.

medium_findings.title: Potential Personal Data Exposure in Error Responses and Logs
medium_findings.description: The skill examples include rejected input values in validation error responses and email addresses in structured logs. This can expose personal data or user-supplied secrets if teams copy the examples without redaction.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly include rejectedValue and email fields in output paths. The risk depends on implementation, but the guidance can be copied into production error handling and logging.

dangerous_patterns.title: Logging or Returning User-Controlled Values
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples show user-controlled or personal values being returned or logged. These values should be redacted, hashed, or omitted in production guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly propagate rejected input and email values. This is a common privacy and compliance risk in error handling documentation.

content.value_statement: Backend teams often create inconsistent exceptions, response bodies, and logging behavior across services. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a concrete Allra pattern for Java error handling.
actual_capabilities: Defines a BusinessException hierarchy for Java services.
Shows ErrorCode enum patterns with HTTP status codes.
Guides Spring Boot global exception handler implementation.
Specifies a standard error response shape.
Demonstrates service-layer exception use with repositories.
Provides logging level guidance for business and system errors.
limitations: It is guidance documentation, not a runnable library.
It focuses on Java and Spring Boot style backends.
It does not validate code against a live Allra repository.
Some logging examples need privacy review before production use.

use_cases.title: Create Consistent Service Exceptions
use_cases.description: Generate custom exception classes that follow the Allra BusinessException and ErrorCode pattern.

use_cases.title: Implement Global API Error Handling
use_cases.description: Build a Spring Boot controller advice that maps business, validation, and unexpected exceptions to standard responses.

use_cases.title: Review Error Handling Standards
use_cases.description: Check service-layer code for consistent exception throwing, response formats, and logging levels.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Exception
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one domain exception.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Java custom exception for a missing order using the Allra error handling standard.

prompt_templates.title: Add Error Codes
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for adding new business errors.
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend the ErrorCode enum for payment failure, duplicate account, and forbidden account access.

prompt_templates.title: Implement Controller Advice
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for API error response handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a Spring Boot global exception handler that follows the Allra standard and avoids leaking sensitive values.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Error Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for reviewing existing service behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this service and controller error flow against the Allra standard. Identify inconsistent exceptions, response shapes, and logging risks.

output_examples.input: Ask for a missing user exception.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes a domain exception that extends the shared business exception base and maps to the user-not-found error code.
The assistant describes a controller advice flow that collects field errors, returns the standard response shape, and avoids exposing sensitive rejected values.
The assistant recommends warn logs for expected business failures and error logs for unexpected system failures, with redaction for personal data.

best_practices: Use one shared error code catalog for each service boundary.
Redact personal data, tokens, passwords, and rejected secrets in logs and responses.
Handle expected business exceptions separately from unexpected system exceptions.
anti_patterns: Do not return stack traces or internal exception details to API clients.
Do not log raw user input, credentials, tokens, or full personal identifiers.
Do not create unrelated response formats for each controller.

faq.question: Does this skill provide runnable code?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation patterns and examples for Java backend projects.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it target?
faq.answer: It targets Java services that use Spring Boot style controllers, services, and exception handlers.

faq.question: Can I use it outside Allra projects?
faq.answer: Yes, but you should adapt error codes, messages, and logging rules to your project standards.

faq.question: Does it handle security exceptions?
faq.answer: It includes unauthorized and forbidden examples, but it is not a full authentication framework.

faq.question: Should rejected values be returned in validation errors?
faq.answer: Use caution. Redact or omit values that may contain personal data, secrets, or sensitive business information.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex apply this during code review?
faq.answer: Yes. They can compare Java error handling code against the documented exception, response, and logging patterns.
